text:
```json
[
  "The document discusses four memory allocation schemes: Paged allocation, Demand paging allocation, Segmented allocation, and Segmented/demand paged allocation.",
  "These memory allocation schemes remove the restriction of storing programs contiguously.",
  "They also eliminate the requirement that the entire program reside in memory during execution.",
  "In paged memory allocation, an incoming job is divided into pages of equal size.",
  "A sector or block is defined as a section of a disk.",
  "A page frame is defined as a section of main memory.",
  "For efficiency, pages, sectors, and page frames are designed to be of the same size.",
  "One sector holds one page of job instruction and fits into one page frame of memory.",
  "Before executing a job, the Memory Manager determines the number of pages, locates enough empty page frames, and loads the pages non-contiguously.",
  "A disadvantage of paged memory allocation is that the Memory Manager must keep track of program pages, which increases OS size and complexity.",
  "The Memory Manager uses three tables to track pages: Job table (JT), Page map table (PMT), and Memory map table (MMT).",
  "Demand paging loads only a part of the program into memory for processing.",
  "In demand paging, a job's equally sized pages initially reside in secondary storage and are brought into memory as needed.",
  "Demand paging allows users to run jobs with less main memory.",
  "Demand paging requires a high-speed direct access storage device that can work directly with the CPU for quick page swapping.",
  "Page swapping in demand paging depends on pre-defined policies.",
  "The page replacement policy is crucial to the efficiency of a demand paging system.",
  "Two popular page replacement policies are First-in first-out (FIFO) and Least-recently-used (LRU).",
  "The clock page replacement policy is a variation of LRU, implemented as a circular queue.",
  "In the clock policy, a page is selected for replacement if its reference bit is 0.",
  "If a page's reference bit is 1 in the clock policy, it is given a second chance and its reference bit is set to 0.",
  "Another LRU variation uses an 8-bit reference and a bit shifting technique to track page usage.",
  "When a page is first copied into memory using this LRU variation, its leftmost bit is set to 1, and other bits are 0.",
  "At each time tick, the Memory Manager shifts every page’s reference byte to the right by one.",
  "Each time a page is referenced, its leftmost bit is set to 1, providing a history of page usage.",
  "The Memory Manager needs specific information about each page in memory to swap pages out.",
  "A Page Map Table for a job includes a Status Bit, Reference Bit, Modified Bit, and Page Frame number.",
  "A page fault occurs when a requested page is not found in memory.",
  "The working set of a job is the set of pages in memory that can be accessed directly without a page fault.",
  "Loading all pages of a job’s working set can minimize page faults and speed up processing.",
  "Identifying each job’s working set and loading it entirely into memory before execution is a solution to working set management.",
  "In time-sharing systems, the OS must track the size and identity of each working set.",
  "Some operating systems use a variable working set size, adjusting it as necessary.",
  "Demand paging introduced virtual memory, allowing jobs to exceed physical memory size.",
  "Demand paging utilizes memory more efficiently by loading job sections only when needed.",
  "A major disadvantage of demand paging is increased overhead from tables and page interrupts.",
  "In segmented memory allocation, jobs are divided into variable-sized segments, each containing related functions.",
  "Segmented allocation reduces page faults caused by segment loops spanning multiple pages.",
  "In segmented memory allocation, main memory is not divided into page frames and is allocated dynamically.",
  "The Memory Manager tracks segments using a Job Table, Segment Map Table, and Memory Map Table.",
  "Pages are fixed-size physical units invisible to the user, while segments are variable-size logical units visible to the user.",
  "Segmented/demand paged memory allocation combines the logical benefits of segmentation with the physical benefits of paging.",
  "In this combined approach, a segment is sub-divided into equal-sized pages, which are easier to manipulate.",
  "This combined approach overcomes segmentation problems like compaction, external fragmentation, and secondary storage handling.",
  "Segmented/demand paged memory allocation requires four tables: Job Table, Segment Map Table, Page Map Table, and Memory Map Table.",
  "Disadvantages of segmented/demand paged allocation include overhead from extra tables and time required to reference SMT and PMT.",
  "Associative memory is used in segmented/demand paged allocation to minimize references and speed up the process.",
  "Associative memory is a set of registers allocated to each active job, residing in main memory.",
  "Associative registers link segments and page numbers of the processed job with their main memory addresses.",
  "When a job is allocated to the CPU, its SMT is loaded into main memory, and its PMT is loaded as needed.",
  "All tables are updated as pages are swapped between main memory and secondary storage.",
  "When a page is first requested, the job’s SMT is searched to locate its PMT, which is then loaded and searched.",
  "If a requested page is not in memory, a page interrupt is issued, the page is brought into memory, and the table is updated.",
  "Associative memory stores information about most-recently-used pages to overcome the time-consuming access of SMT and PMT.",
  "When a page request is issued, searches are initiated through both SMT/PMT and associative registers.",
  "If the associative register search is successful, table searches stop, and address translation uses associative register information.",
  "Virtual memory enables swapping pages between main memory and secondary storage.",
  "Virtual memory gives users the impression that their entire program is loaded in main memory, even if only a portion is.",
  "Before virtual memory, programmers had to ensure programs fit into available physical memory.",
  "Virtual memory with paging allows internal fragmentation but not external fragmentation, using equal-sized pages and a PMT.",
  "Virtual memory with segmentation allows external fragmentation but not internal fragmentation, using unequal-sized segments and an SMT.",
  "Segmentation in virtual memory allows users to share program code.",
  "A shared segment contains unchangeable (reentrant) code and multiple data areas, one for each user.",
  "Virtual memory involves cooperation between the Memory Manager and processor hardware.",
  "Virtual memory management allows a job’s size to not be restricted by main memory size.",
  "Virtual memory uses memory more efficiently by storing only immediately needed job sections.",
  "Virtual memory management allows an unlimited amount of multiprogramming.",
  "Virtual memory management eliminates external fragmentation and minimizes internal fragmentation by combining segmentation and paging.",
  "Virtual memory management allows sharing of code and data.",
  "Virtual memory management facilitates dynamic linking of program segments."
]
```
json_data: The document discusses four memory allocation schemes: Paged allocation, Demand paging allocation, Segmented allocation, and Segmented/demand paged allocation.
These memory allocation schemes remove the restriction of storing programs contiguously.
They also eliminate the requirement that the entire program reside in memory during execution.
In paged memory allocation, an incoming job is divided into pages of equal size.
A sector or block is defined as a section of a disk.
A page frame is defined as a section of main memory.
For efficiency, pages, sectors, and page frames are designed to be of the same size.
One sector holds one page of job instruction and fits into one page frame of memory.
Before executing a job, the Memory Manager determines the number of pages, locates enough empty page frames, and loads the pages non-contiguously.
A disadvantage of paged memory allocation is that the Memory Manager must keep track of program pages, which increases OS size and complexity.
The Memory Manager uses three tables to track pages: Job table (JT), Page map table (PMT), and Memory map table (MMT).
Demand paging loads only a part of the program into memory for processing.
In demand paging, a job's equally sized pages initially reside in secondary storage and are brought into memory as needed.
Demand paging allows users to run jobs with less main memory.
Demand paging requires a high-speed direct access storage device that can work directly with the CPU for quick page swapping.
Page swapping in demand paging depends on pre-defined policies.
The page replacement policy is crucial to the efficiency of a demand paging system.
Two popular page replacement policies are First-in first-out (FIFO) and Least-recently-used (LRU).
The clock page replacement policy is a variation of LRU, implemented as a circular queue.
In the clock policy, a page is selected for replacement if its reference bit is 0.
If a page's reference bit is 1 in the clock policy, it is given a second chance and its reference bit is set to 0.
Another LRU variation uses an 8-bit reference and a bit shifting technique to track page usage.
When a page is first copied into memory using this LRU variation, its leftmost bit is set to 1, and other bits are 0.
At each time tick, the Memory Manager shifts every page’s reference byte to the right by one.
Each time a page is referenced, its leftmost bit is set to 1, providing a history of page usage.
The Memory Manager needs specific information about each page in memory to swap pages out.
A Page Map Table for a job includes a Status Bit, Reference Bit, Modified Bit, and Page Frame number.
A page fault occurs when a requested page is not found in memory.
The working set of a job is the set of pages in memory that can be accessed directly without a page fault.
Loading all pages of a job’s working set can minimize page faults and speed up processing.
Identifying each job’s working set and loading it entirely into memory before execution is a solution to working set management.
In time-sharing systems, the OS must track the size and identity of each working set.
Some operating systems use a variable working set size, adjusting it as necessary.
Demand paging introduced virtual memory, allowing jobs to exceed physical memory size.
Demand paging utilizes memory more efficiently by loading job sections only when needed.
A major disadvantage of demand paging is increased overhead from tables and page interrupts.
In segmented memory allocation, jobs are divided into variable-sized segments, each containing related functions.
Segmented allocation reduces page faults caused by segment loops spanning multiple pages.
In segmented memory allocation, main memory is not divided into page frames and is allocated dynamically.
The Memory Manager tracks segments using a Job Table, Segment Map Table, and Memory Map Table.
Pages are fixed-size physical units invisible to the user, while segments are variable-size logical units visible to the user.
Segmented/demand paged memory allocation combines the logical benefits of segmentation with the physical benefits of paging.
In this combined approach, a segment is sub-divided into equal-sized pages, which are easier to manipulate.
This combined approach overcomes segmentation problems like compaction, external fragmentation, and secondary storage handling.
Segmented/demand paged memory allocation requires four tables: Job Table, Segment Map Table, Page Map Table, and Memory Map Table.
Disadvantages of segmented/demand paged allocation include overhead from extra tables and time required to reference SMT and PMT.
Associative memory is used in segmented/demand paged allocation to minimize references and speed up the process.
Associative memory is a set of registers allocated to each active job, residing in main memory.
Associative registers link segments and page numbers of the processed job with their main memory addresses.
When a job is allocated to the CPU, its SMT is loaded into main memory, and its PMT is loaded as needed.
All tables are updated as pages are swapped between main memory and secondary storage.
When a page is first requested, the job’s SMT is searched to locate its PMT, which is then loaded and searched.
If a requested page is not in memory, a page interrupt is issued, the page is brought into memory, and the table is updated.
Associative memory stores information about most-recently-used pages to overcome the time-consuming access of SMT and PMT.
When a page request is issued, searches are initiated through both SMT/PMT and associative registers.
If the associative register search is successful, table searches stop, and address translation uses associative register information.
Virtual memory enables swapping pages between main memory and secondary storage.
Virtual memory gives users the impression that their entire program is loaded in main memory, even if only a portion is.
Before virtual memory, programmers had to ensure programs fit into available physical memory.
Virtual memory with paging allows internal fragmentation but not external fragmentation, using equal-sized pages and a PMT.
Virtual memory with segmentation allows external fragmentation but not internal fragmentation, using unequal-sized segments and an SMT.
Segmentation in virtual memory allows users to share program code.
A shared segment contains unchangeable (reentrant) code and multiple data areas, one for each user.
Virtual memory involves cooperation between the Memory Manager and processor hardware.
Virtual memory management allows a job’s size to not be restricted by main memory size.
Virtual memory uses memory more efficiently by storing only immediately needed job sections.
Virtual memory management allows an unlimited amount of multiprogramming.
Virtual memory management eliminates external fragmentation and minimizes internal fragmentation by combining segmentation and paging.
Virtual memory management allows sharing of code and data.
Virtual memory management facilitates dynamic linking of program segments.
